posts.text: Some tasks don't get easier the longer you wait. They just get heavier. The weight isn't the task. It's everything you've added to it by avoiding it.

posts.text:
I put on a cheap yellow jumpsuit and a respirator.

Crawled under my house.

Found a rotting opossum corpse.

Then had to drag it out on a snow shovel while maggots scattered.

Here's what a dead opossum taught me about productivity 👇

The smell showed up first.

I explored every other option. Found mushrooms under the deck. Hoped against hope.

But I already knew.

I just didn't want to deal with it.

So I waited.

All waiting did was let the dread build. The guilt compound. The smell get worse.

First step into the crawl space — I ripped a hole in the crotch of the jumpsuit.

Instant vulnerability.

Crawled on hands and knees in the dirt.

Finally made eye contact with the empty sockets of a skull in the corner.

Quest accomplished. Except now I had to get it out.

Emerged dirty, sweaty, and emotionally spent.

Buried it in the greenbelt in a ripped yellow hazmat suit and respirator.

Probably terrified my neighbors.

Then showered. Watched a movie with my wife. Let the ridiculousness of it sink in.

And I felt genuinely fine.

Here's what I noticed:

The task itself was always going to be awful.

Every day I avoided it, I didn't reduce that cost.

I just added guilt and worry on top of it.

The opossum didn't get less dead. My dread just got bigger.

Avoiding hard tasks doesn't protect you from them.

The restorative plan after the hard thing matters as much as doing the hard thing.

Shower. Movie. Wife. Decompress.

Not a reward. A recovery.

When you map the emotional cost of a task before you start it — you can plan what you need after.

That's the whole game.

Full system breakdown in this week's dev log 👇

The emotional cost of avoided tasks compounds daily. Pay it early or pay it with interest.

Thanks for reading.
I'm Nick — a dad of two building a board game about managing life without losing yourself in the process.

posts.text:
Avoiding a hard task feels like protecting yourself.

It isn't.

It's a tax. And it compounds daily.

Here's what nobody tells you about procrastination 👇

When you skip a task you don't want to do:

The task stays the same size.

The dread gets bigger.
The guilt gets louder.
The mental energy cost goes up.

You didn't defer the cost. You inflated it.

Avoidance doesn't protect you. It charges you interest.

The standard advice: just do the hard thing.

That's not wrong. It's just incomplete.

Because the hard thing has an emotional cost attached to it.

And if you don't plan for that cost — you'll keep avoiding it.

Not from laziness. From self-preservation instinct.

The better question isn't "why won't I do this?"

It's "what does this task actually cost me, and what do I need after?"

Map the emotional weight. Plan the recovery.

Then the hard thing becomes manageable.

Not easy. Manageable.

I crawled under my house to remove a decomposing opossum.

I'd been avoiding it for days.

The task took 45 minutes.

The avoidance cost me a week of low-grade dread.

Pay early or pay with interest.

That's the whole lesson.

The version of you that finally does the thing costs less than the version that keeps delaying it.

Thanks for reading.
I'm Nick — a dad of two building a board game about managing life without losing yourself in the process.

posts.text:
Most tasks that feel impossible aren't impossible.

They're emotionally expensive.

And you're trying to start them without knowing what they actually cost.

Here's a simple tool I built to fix that 👇

Step 1: Name the feeling — not the task.

Not "I need to clean the garage."

"I need to clean the garage and it makes me feel overwhelmed and resentful."

That feeling is the real obstacle.

You can't solve a problem you haven't named.

Step 2: Map the emotional weight.

Ask: What specifically makes this hard?

Disgust? Anxiety? Overwhelm? Boredom?

Once you see it clearly, it has less power over you.

And you can build a plan around it instead of fighting it blind.

Step 3: Plan the recovery before you start.

Not after. Before.

Decide what you need when it's done.

A shower. A walk. 20 minutes with no obligations.

Knowing recovery is waiting makes starting easier.

You're not enduring the task. You're completing a full loop.

Step 4: Move anyway — even partially.

You don't have to finish it today.

Every attempt gives you more information about what makes it hard.

Progress isn't completion. It's forward momentum in a direction that feels sustainable.

One shovel of dirt is still one shovel closer.

Name the feeling. Map the cost. Plan recovery. Move anyway. That's it.

Thanks for reading.
I'm Nick — a dad of two building a board game about managing life without losing yourself in the process.

posts.text:
Dr. Russell Barkley has spent decades studying ADHD.

One of his most important observations:

People with ADHD don't have a problem knowing what to do.

They have a problem starting things when the emotional cost feels too high.

That distinction changes everything 👇

Standard advice treats procrastination as a discipline problem.

Barkley treats it as an emotional regulation problem.

The task isn't too hard. The emotional weight attached to it is too high to initiate.

Fix the weight problem and the initiation problem often solves itself.

BJ Fogg's behavior design research points to the same place from a different direction.

Motivation is unreliable.

But clarity — knowing exactly what you're doing and what comes after — dramatically lowers the activation cost.

Plan the recovery. Lower the cost. Start more often.

The insight both researchers circle without naming directly:

You need an emotional exit before you'll take an emotional entrance.

If the task has no clear end — not a finish line, but a decompression plan — your brain will protect you from starting it.

That's not weakness. That's the system working as designed.

What this means practically:

Before the hard task, name what makes it hard.

Then name what comes after.

Not a reward. A recovery.

Your brain needs to know it won't be left holding the cost forever.

Give it a way out and it'll give you a way in.

The obstacle isn't the task. It's the unplanned emotional cost sitting on the other side of starting.

Thanks for reading.
I'm Nick — a dad of two building a board game about managing life without losing yourself in the process.

posts.text:
I used to feel low-level dread about certain tasks for days.

Couldn't explain it. Couldn't shake it.

Just this ambient weight sitting on everything.

I didn't understand what I was actually feeling until I started building the game 👇

Here's what I found:

The task wasn't the problem.

The unexamined emotion attached to the task was the problem.

Disgust. Anxiety. Overwhelm. Dread.

All of it sitting inside me with no name and no external target.

Internal weight is the hardest kind to carry.

The game has a mechanic I'm building called the Quest Matrix.

The idea: map hard tasks onto monsters that represent what you're actually feeling.

Not a metaphor for fun. A tool for externalizing the emotional weight.

Once the feeling is outside you, it's easier to face.

Past me avoided the dead opossum under the house for days.

Not because I didn't know it needed to happen.

Because I hadn't named what made it feel so heavy.

Disgust. Dread. Physical vulnerability.

Naming it would have made it smaller.

Avoiding it made it bigger.

The shift I'm building toward:

Tasks don't carry hidden costs when you've already looked at the costs directly.

Name the emotion. Plan the recovery.

The monster doesn't disappear but it stops living rent-free in your chest.

That's worth more than any productivity tip I've ever read.

Seeing the emotional cost of a task clearly is the first move toward defeating it.

Thanks for reading.
I'm Nick — a dad of two building a board game about managing life without losing yourself in the process.

posts.text:
New mechanic just made it into the game.

It came from a dead opossum, a ripped hazmat suit, and a snow shovel.

Here's what I built and why 👇

The game already tracks energy states and task types.

But it was missing something.

The emotional cost of a task before you start it.

Not the time cost. Not the effort cost.

The feeling cost.

That's the real reason hard tasks don't get done.

The Quest Matrix maps tasks onto monsters.

Each monster represents a feeling cluster: disgust, dread, overwhelm, anxiety.

You name what you're facing. You plan the restorative quest that follows.

Then you move.

External enemy. Visible cost. Recovery plan baked in.

The invisible becomes manageable.

What this changes in the game:

Players aren't just managing time and energy anymore.

They're managing emotional load across the day.

Hard quests have monsters. Monsters have counters. Counters become the plan.

The system holds the emotional context so your brain doesn't have to.

What I'm still figuring out:

How granular does the monster taxonomy need to be?

Is disgust different enough from dread to warrant a separate creature?

Does naming the emotion matter more than categorizing it precisely?

Still in v1. Still building.

But the mechanic is in and it changes how the whole system feels.

Every mechanic in this game exists because a real situation made the gap visible.

Thanks for reading.
I'm Nick — a dad of two building a board game about managing life without losing yourself in the process.

posts.text:
Some tasks feel impossible even though they're not actually hard.

The problem isn't the task. It's the emotional cost you haven't accounted for.

Here are 5 types — and what each one actually needs 👇

1. The Disgust Task

Crawling under houses. Deep cleaning. Anything involving decay.

What it needs: A clear endpoint and a recovery plan.

Decide what you're doing immediately after. A shower. A walk. Something clean.

Knowing the recovery is waiting makes starting tolerable.

2. The Dread Task

Phone calls you've been putting off. Difficult conversations. Medical appointments.

What it needs: Time-boxing and a witness.

Set a hard end time. Tell someone you're doing it.

Dread shrinks when it has a boundary and an audience.

3. The Overwhelm Task

Anything with too many moving pieces and no clear first step.

What it needs: A single visible starting action.

Not a plan. Just step one.

Overwhelm is almost always a missing entry point, not a missing ability.

4. The Resentment Task

Things you're doing because you feel like you have to.

What it needs: A choice reframe.

You're not doing it because you must. You're doing it because of what it protects.

Choosing your reason doesn't change the task. It changes your relationship to it.

5. The Anxiety Task

Anything with uncertain outcomes. High stakes. Judgment from others.

What it needs: Permission to do it badly.

Anxiety tasks don't need perfection. They need a first attempt.

Done badly still moves the needle. Undone doesn't move anything.

Name the emotional type. Plan for what it actually costs. Then move.

Thanks for reading.
I'm Nick — a dad of two building a board game about managing life without losing yourself in the process.
